audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua twelve-stone memorial and Jordan-exit narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for twelve tribal stones, child-question catechesis, priestly exit, armed Transjordan contingent, Joshua's elevation, Gilgal memorial, Exodus comparison, and public-sign theology.

verses.restored: It was, when all nation finished crossing Jordan, that YHWH spoke to Joshua, saying,
Take for yourselves twelve men from people, one man from each tribe,

and command them, saying, Lift up for yourselves from here, from midst of Jordan, from standing place of priests' feet, twelve stones; carry them across with you and set them down in lodging place where you lodge tonight.
Joshua called twelve men whom he appointed from children of Israel, one man from each tribe.
Joshua said to them, Cross before ark of YHWH your Divine into midst of Jordan, and lift up each man one stone upon his shoulder, according to number of tribes of children of Israel,
so this may be a sign in your midst. When your children ask tomorrow, saying, What are these stones to you?

then you shall say to them, Waters of Jordan were cut off before ark of covenant of YHWH; when it crossed Jordan, waters of Jordan were cut off. These stones shall be as memorial for children of Israel forever.

Children of Israel did so as Joshua commanded; they lifted twelve stones from midst of Jordan, as YHWH spoke to Joshua, according to number of tribes of children of Israel, carried them across to lodging place, and set them down there.
Joshua raised twelve stones in midst of Jordan, under standing place of feet of priests carrying ark of covenant, and they are there to this day.
Priests carrying ark stood in midst of Jordan until every word was completed that YHWH commanded Joshua to speak to people, according to all Moses commanded Joshua; and people hurried and crossed.
It was, when all people finished crossing, that ark of YHWH and priests crossed before people.
Sons of Reuben, sons of Gad, and half tribe of Manasseh crossed armed before children of Israel, as Moses spoke to them.
About forty thousand equipped for host crossed before YHWH for battle to plains of Jericho.
On that day YHWH made Joshua great in eyes of all Israel, and they feared/revered him as they feared/revered Moses all days of his life.
YHWH spoke to Joshua, saying,
Command priests carrying ark of testimony that they come up from Jordan.
Joshua commanded priests, saying, Come up from Jordan.

It was, when priests carrying ark of covenant of YHWH came up from midst of Jordan and soles of priests' feet were drawn onto dry ground, that waters of Jordan returned to their place and went over all its banks as before.
People came up from Jordan on tenth day of first month and camped at Gilgal on eastern edge of Jericho.
Those twelve stones they took from Jordan Joshua raised at Gilgal.
He spoke to children of Israel, saying, When your children ask their fathers tomorrow, saying, What are these stones?
you shall make your children know, saying, On dry ground Israel crossed this Jordan.
For YHWH your Divine dried waters of Jordan before you until you crossed, as YHWH your Divine did to Sea of Reeds, which he dried before us until we crossed,
so all peoples of earth may know hand of YHWH, that it is strong, and so you may fear/revere YHWH your Divine all days.

translation_notes: The memorial is deliberately intergenerational: stones exist to provoke children's questions rather than to silence them.
Two sets of twelve stones may be implied—one carried to Gilgal and one set in the river—though textual and archaeological interpretation remains debated.
The priests remain in the river until all people and all commanded actions are complete, preserving the theme that sacred responsibility stays with the vulnerable crossing.
The armed Transjordan contingent and forty-thousand figure belong to conquest mobilization and require numerical/textual audit.
Joshua's elevation and the people's fear/reverence mirror Moses, but public legitimacy must not become personality worship.
The Jordan crossing is compared to the Sea of Reeds, extending Exodus memory into conquest. Liberation memory must not be used to sanctify another people's dispossession.
Public memorials are ethically responsible only when they tell the truth about both the community's survival and the people endangered by its triumph.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 4 to build nationalist monuments that erase victims, glorify military conquest, or turn reverence for leaders into unquestioning obedience. Center children's questions, truthful public memory, shared tribal witness, and sacred presence that remains until everyone crosses.
